sentences.text: Yeah, so I told corporal to take two of our men, take the jeep, bring her in, and we'll see.
So I check the papers.
She showed me a pass, which the camp commander had signed, given her, giving her permission to go to [INAUDIBLE].
No, I don't want that one.
I want to SS Auschweiss, which was a little bit bigger than a credit card.
And [INAUDIBLE].
They're all [INAUDIBLE] or whatever, or 1923.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I said, well, we better-- we'll detain her for 48 hours.
Maybe someone will come forward.
So she said, can she go home to collect some personal belongings like brush, a comb, toothbrush?
She wasn't short of anything.
So I said, we'll take an armed guard.
I gave strict instructions, an armed guard.
I said, you take her to home.
If she tries to make a run for it, you shoot to kill.
Because we had all this.
If you was escorting soldiers, and they tried to make a run for it during transit, you shoot to wound them, like in the leg or something.
I said, you shoot to kill.
Don't hesitate.
I'll take full responsibility for it.
She went home.
She didn't give us any trouble.
So we brought her back.
I went through the case.
She had a case.
She had a neatly-- she's got a black skirt from the SS uniform.
She had all cosmetics, hairbrush, not short of anything.
I said, take her back into the, into the cells.
And tell the cook, tell the cook to give her a tin of corned beef.
That's got  to last her 48 hours.
Now that-- and I said to the [? woman, ?] now go back.
If you find any friends, anyone, stand on the rooftops.
See if you can find two-- I must have two witnesses that actually saw her do it.
Otherwise, it won't stick.
So she went out there [INAUDIBLE].
They took her back into the cells.
A few minutes later, someone comes back.
He says, haven't got a tin opener to open the-- of course, some tins-- this was before the age of ring pull.
Ring pull cans wasn't invented yet.
And some tins, like sardines, had a key in it.
And some didn't.
And some corned beef tins had the key on it.
And some didn't.
This one didn't.
So he comes in.
He says, have you got a key to open the corned beef.
I says no, I haven't.
He says, can you get one?
I says, there's nothing in the army instructions about keys for corned beef.
All it says is, one can of corned beef for 48 hours during transit.
And that's what she's got.
There's nothing about giving tin openers.
You want a tin opener, you go outside and ask the, the people walking about.
Tell them you want a tin opener for an SS officer.
So he said-- so he starts losing his temper.
He says, you can't do that under the Geneva Convention.
I said, no, you listen to me.
Under the Geneva Convention, she's not entitled to anything.
Because under the Geneva Convention, it's for the German armed forces.
She's not a member of the German armed forces.
She's a member of the SS, which is a political organization.
So don't tell me what to do.
She's not entitled to it.
She's not covered by it.
So he went out.
Then what happened?
So that episode was finished.
I went off duty, waiting for someone to come in that seen witnesses.
Nothing is happening.
Then I get called-- you're wanted in the office.
What is it?
You'll find out quick enough.
Went back into the office.
The colonel of the camp was there, the camp-- like British officer.
Are you the soldier that canceled my pass?
I said, what?
They want Irma Grese to go to the cellar?
He says, if I give a pass, I'm lieutenant colonel of this camp, camp commandant.
Who are you to cancel that authority?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I said, so I  said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- I thought she's, she's not, she's not entitled to it.
He says, if I give a pass, I don't expect anybody to cancel.
What, what authority have you got to cancel it?
So with that, the sergeant chimes in.
Oh, and he threw his identity card down.
He says, that's my authority, lieutenant colonel, I think his name was [?
Byrd ?].
I've been all my lifetime in the army.
And that's my-- and he threw it down like that.
And he said, that's my authority.
So the sergeant said, that's your authority?
Well, I'll trump it here with my authority.
He threw down a warrant card.
And I had a warrant card as well.
And every policeman, military or civilian, carries what was known as a warrant card, which gives you the authority to do whatever you think in the line of duty.
I don't know who signs the civilian, maybe the commissioner for police, or maybe [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know.
So I threw that.
I says, that's my authority.
And I threw down my warrant card.
Apparently, he hadn't seen it.
So he read it.
So the sergeant said, well, if you just sign this form, I'll forward it on to the provost marshal.
And we'll convene a court of inquiry.
So he said, you know fully well, if I sign that form, I sign away my commission.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What it said on the warrant card, it said, it's got your name, rank, and number.
And it said, do not, under any circumstances, allow yourself to be bullied or intimidated by any rank, up to and included the rank of a major general.
Now there's only two ranks higher, a lieutenant general and a full general.
After that, a field marshal is an honorary rank.
So what happened to--

So-- and it said, don't allow yourself to be bullied or intimidated, or different things, if you think, in the line of duty, you're acting in a correct and proper matter, signed Bernard Law Montgomery, Commander in Chief, 21st Armored Group, Field Marshal.
You wish to argue with that?
I said, I'll lodge a complaint to the provost marshal.
So he walked out.
So we kept her for 48 hours.
And no one come, no one come forward.
Sergeant said to me, he said, look, we've had the 48 hours.
No  one has come forward.
We've got to let her go.
Everybody in the camp knew she was being held.
And everybody knew who she was.
He said, but you can't make a claim.
You can't report her for anything.
There's no witnesses.
I says, look.
Give me another 24 hours.
Give me till tomorrow, half past four, which is tea time.
See, we, we could bend the rules if we wanted to.
The rules were 48 hours a prisoner, and then either you let them go, or you write a report out.
I said, give me to tomorrow, half past four, tea time.
I no one comes for her by tomorrow, half past four, we have to let her go.
Because no one is going to come forward [INAUDIBLE].
Yes, he says, all right, you've got a deal.
Tomorrow, half past four, if no one comes forward, she goes.
Right.
Normally, the tour of duty is two hours on, four hours off.
But we altered it like three hours on.
And we had six hours off.
So six hours off is better than four hours so we don't have to get up in the middle of the night sometimes.
So he said, all right, if no one comes forward by tomorrow at 4:30, she goes.
So we kept her again.
[INAUDIBLE] no one came forward.
The following day, the third day, no one came forward.
I was on duty, 1 o'clock.
I'd give it up already.
Because if no one comes forward in two and a half days, there's no one, no one there.
3 o'clock, 4 o'clock, nothing, half past four, no one-- [INAUDIBLE] half past four, that was the deal, half past four.
I went off 5 o'clock.
About 10 to five, I start sweeping up the office.
Of course, the night guard took over.
[INAUDIBLE] We're tidying up the report books.
And about three minutes to five, two girls walked in, two sisters.
Have I got a, a job for them?
They speak very good English.
They heard that the military police need interpreters.
I says, we're 17 people.
We've got-- we're doing rations for 35.
Everybody's got a, an interpreter.
And one has got a cousin, a little boy of 10.
He's also on the rations.
I says, we can't take anymore on.
I says, I'm sorry.
But I didn't want to let them go out with nothing, like empty handed.
I said, look, it's 5 o'clock.
I'm going off now.
I'm going in for tea 5 o'clock.
If you like, you can come in.
I'll ask the cook to make you tea, same as we're getting.
They said, yeah, fine, good.
Of course, you couldn't get anything to eat, only what-- they were getting the, the rations.
So they kicked up a bit of a fuss.
I said, oh, they made a fuss.
Pour more water in the tea, will you?
So anyway, I think it was Welsh rarebit at the time.
So he made them tea.
I said, the dining room was upstairs.
So I said, we'll go upstairs.
And I started talking  to her, one thing or another, what they were doing, where they come from.
So we go upstairs.
And there's three seats in the far corner.
So I said, well, look, we'll sit over there.
There's three, three empty seats.
So she was the younger, she was the younger one.
I think she was the younger one.
She says she's not going to sit over there.
I says, look, I'm sorry I can't offer you the same services as the Hotel [INAUDIBLE], which is the German equivalent to the English Savoy.
I says, I can't offer you the same-- you either take it, or you don't take it.
I'm sorry.
She said, no, no, no.
No, we're very grateful that you give us tea.
You give us something to eat.
But, she said, surely you don't expect us to sit next to an SS woman, an SS officer.
So I said, you know, I suppose you're right there.
I says, I know she's an SS officer.
I know she made the selections at Auschwitz.
But what I don't know is who saw her do it.
That's why we've had to let her go.
We've had her here three days.
And we've got to leave her go.
So she said, well, I saw her do it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when she said that, the mug of tea and the tea fell out my hands.
Here, we've been trying three days, frantically trying to find someone that see her.
And here someone comes in at the last minute and says she's seen her do it.
She's seen her make the selections.
So I said, well, how did you see her make these selections?
So she said, I used to play in the orchestra.
So I looked at her, I said, what orchestra?
She said, there was a string orchestra outside the crematorium by the gas chambers.
To drown the screams of the people, they used to play classical music.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm looking at her, and I said to the sister, did you also play in orchestra?
She said, no, she had a job in the office.
She spoke a few-- English, German, French.
She spoke a few languages, so she used to be an interpreter and run errands for the Germans.
So she used to see through the window in the office when she used to make these selections.
So she said she used to see it.
I said, forget about the tea.
I called over to the fellow she was talking to.
I said, bring her back.
She's going back into the, into the cells in the bunker.
I went downstairs.
I said to the sergeant, get the charge sheet out.
I got two witnesses.
So he said, you're better off-- if they, if they played in the orchestra, it may be just some more people from the orchestra.
Because the more witnesses you've got, the better.
Because the way the people were dying like flies, by the time it gets to court, it can take months before it gets to court.
They might be dead.
Or they go off somewhere.
They might go to America.
They could go anywhere.
And you'd be left with no one.
So they said they had another friend, a little French girl, who she couldn't play.
But they put her in the middle row of the orchestra.
And she made   a noise.
So they covered up for it.
That was a big risk on their part.
They put her in the middle.
She made a noise as well.
So she-- but after seven days, she went back to Paris.
She wasn't going to hang it back for trial coming up in a few months' time.
But these two sisters, they stayed behind.
For some reason-- they, they come from Germany.
So maybe they had nowhere to go.
So they stayed behind.
So I took her back.
And I went back.
And I filled out the report.
We sent the report in to the provost marshal, provost marshal.
He sent it in to the war crimes commission, which was there.
And they formally charged her.
And she got charged with Kramer.
I think it was November.
She got executed with Kramer.
Any other famous people did you meet, did you come across, or infamous people?
No.
After that-- then that was, was getting towards September already.
And I went over for Rosh Hashanah.
I did want her to go.
But by then-- oh yes.
There was a cinema in the barracks, which the Germans used to go.
In their spare time, they used to go to the cinema.
They used to pay to go in, admission.
And I went down there.
We, we went into the cinema.
So someone comes up-- German, he was-- about 16 stone, mouth full of gold teeth.
And I said, who are you?
He says he manages the cinema.
I said, you manage  the cinema?
So who does it belong to?
He says, it belongs to my sister-in law, the world famous Marlene Dietrich.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I thought, it doesn't add up, if, if-- Sorry, where actually, is this cinema?
In the barracks, in the tank training [INAUDIBLE], in--
In Bergen.
It was about six kilometers away from the original camp, where the SS used to go to, to their entertainment.
So I said, it doesn't seem to add up.
Where do you live?
He had a luxury flat above the cinema.
We went up.
We  went to see.
He had a luxury flat.
He had a Steinway baby grand up there.
He was living the life of Riley.
And on the door, there was a--
Yeah.
George Wills.
German, he was German.
Yes, and when I went upstairs in the projection room, there was bundles of papers written him, why I'm a Nazi.
There was bundles, hundreds of them there.
I should have taken them.
But you didn't think of these things at the time.
And there was three projectors.
So I say to him-- ?] one was a spare.
And two was for showing the films.
And the projectionist was a Yugoslav prisoner of war.
He was a radio operator in the submarine service.
And he was a prisoner of war.
Pardon?
Herman ?] [?
Veranik. ?] He came from northern, well, then, Yugoslavia.
And he, he knew how to operate these project-- the machine-- projection-- projections.
So he knew what was going on.
So he said, she's got four cinemas in Germany, not just this one.
He spoke good English.
He said, she's got this one here, which is a barracks, which is a big tank training place.
She had another one in Fallingbostel.
Fallingbostel is a garrison town like Aldershot.
And it's still, even to this day, NATO has got their troops there in Fallingbostel.
She had another one in Felderfink and another one in [INAUDIBLE].
She had four cinemas.
And they were never requisitioned.
So it's always come to me that there must have been some liaison between her and the German government.
Because she always boasted she's helping the Allied cause.
She used to entertain the American troops.
And, and she never mentions about these cinemas.
And when you read the, the autobiography, the word Belsen is not even mentioned.
She doesn't even mention she had four cinemas.
She doesn't even mention-- she came to Belsen.
She came down once.
There was a fleet of four jeeps come in.
And I was on duty at the time.
And there's another fellow with me.
And on the jeeps, there's no doors.
You have to climb in or climb out.
And there's steps that go up, like in the public buildings.
And as she got out the jeep, he says, oh, he says, there's only one person that's got legs like that.
And that's Marlene Dietrich.
I said, don't be such a fool.
Eejit, ?] what's she doing in a place like this?
He said, don't you tell me.
I might not be a good soldier, but I know a good pair of legs when I see them.
And I'm telling you, that's Marlene Dietrich.
Don't argue.
So anyway, coming back to the, the camp, what other activities were you involved with?
No, it's just that it settled down afterwards.
If someone wanted a burial, like they had a relative or a friend, they wanted an individual burial, they come into us, could we arrange it for them?
It slowed down.
It peaked.
At one time, there  was 1,250 in one day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's when it peaked.
Well, they were put in mass graves in, in the camp, in the original concentration--
They got the Germans to do it.
They rounded up all the German civilians.
And they done it.
And accidentally, sometimes, someone would give them a push into the grave as well.
Yeah.
Well, they said, they didn't know what was going on.
They all said they didn't know.
But you could smell the, the death smell.
True, no one was allowed in the camp.
You wasn't-- as far as you got was-- like tradesman-- you went to the gate.
And that was it.
And anybody who went to the camp never come out.
No.
It's in the middle of a field, like a wood.
It was cut out, with trees all the way around.
If you went past, you wouldn't know it was there.
Apart from the smell, yeah.
But there was trees all the way around.
And you wouldn't know it was there.
You'd just go past it.
I was there till September.
I went there-- September, I went home Rosh Hoshanah.
And my unit moved into Hanover.
In Hanover, well, I wasn't there.
But when I came back, they said, a pity you wasn't here.
The officer, they went ahead of her.
And they had to pick out billets.
So they picked a private street that Max Reinhardt, the theater producer, director, he used to live there.
There were very private houses.
He called the inhabitants together.
He said, you've got four hours to get out.
We're taking over these houses.
So they started to complaining.
They said--
For the troops, for our section.
They said, they can't possibly, in four hours, take out all your belongings if you live in some big luxury house.
So he paused for a moment.
And he said, yes, I suppose you're right.
You can't take it out.
I'll tell you what I'll do.
I'll give you two hours, the same amount of time as you gave the people when you rounded them up for the concentration camps.
You've got two hours to get out.
If you're not out, I'll have you thrown out.
And then I was there for two hours-- oh, for, for a few months, until about Christmas.
And then the units start-- the demobilization started coming in.
So units start amalgamating.
And we went up to the Schleswig-Holstein.
And from there, I went up to Flensburg.
On February 6, 1946.
Well, there was a lot shortages, like for civilians.
But the, the war was finished.
So the lights were on.
And things started to go back to normal.

But when I was in Flensburg-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, it was in Schleswig-Holstein-- we had to do the morning, the daytime guards, the military police, which was from 6 o'clock in the morning to 6 o'clock at night.
Then the night guards took over.
And it was in the what they called the rat house, which was the town hall, which is the main building in the town.
And the Germans, nothing more they like than a band and a uniform.
Give  them a band and a uniform, and a ceremonial show, and you win them over.
So I had to go on duty at 6 o'clock in the morning. 'Reveille' was 6 o'clock.
It was bitterly cold.
So I was told, 6 o'clock, when the clock strikes six in the clock tower, the bugler will sound 'Reveille.' And as he does that, you hoist the Union Jack up on the flagpole.
But make sure the white cross, the narrow white cross goes nearer to the flag pole.
The narrow stripe on the-- the white narrow stripe on the flag goes nearest to the flagpole.
Sure enough, 6 o'clock, I was there.
And there was some Germans going to work.
They had their lunch boxes.
And the clock struck 6 o'clock.
The bugler sounds 'Reveille.' and I pulled up the flag.
And as I pulled up the flag, it suddenly struck me, what would the Germans have said, what they're reaction would have been.
It's bad enough having an enemy flag and an enemy soldier hoisting the flag on one of your prestigious building.
But the soldier that done it was Jewish on top of it.
[LAUGHS] I don't know what they would have thought.
And from there, Schleswig-Holstein, I come back to England.
No-- we wasn't-- I don't think, I don't think it applies to the entire army, but as far as I was, I was concerned, we was not demobilized.
We was released.
And the class said reserve, subject to recall in the case of a national emergency, which I didn't pay any notice to.
And that was in February in 1946.
In 19-- June, 1952, I got a letter from the war office.
You've been recalled for duty.
Go to the local labor exchange for a medical and report to Chatham barracks for two weeks.
Because that was the heart of the Cold War.
And there was a bit of a panic in the war office.
So they wanted to call the reserves up to be in position for, like, important buildings, railways, power stations, waterworks, and docks, and all.
Well, they couldn't care less.
Well, at first, but then, afterwards, it wears off.
And people, they're not interested.
They go, you know, it goes on and on and on.
I mean, the generation today, they might look at it.
And, and then they go on to something else.
It's only the people that were actually there.
I mean, you have society today, like 45 age group, which has got survivors or children-- transport children that were sent over after the war that came to England.
They belong to this.
But the, like the second generation, they are not so interested in it.
It's something happened in history.
They believed you, yes.
Yeah, they believed it.
But the, the whole thing, after a few months, it all died down.
And then it only come to life last year for the 50th anniversary.
Otherwise, nobody ever mentioned a word about it.
It was different than what, what I left.
It was like a different world you come back to.
Things have changed.
New inventions come out.
[INAUDIBLE] Ball pens had come on the scene.
And different typewriters come out.
The old fashioned typewriters had gone.
I was in tailoring.
No, different, different.
Of course, the work had changed.
New machines had come from America.
And the work what you'd done, say, prewar, that had gone by the board.
You, you had all different machines.
Before the war, you never got paid for holidays.
After war, the unions forced it through that everybody should get two weeks' holiday with pay, which you never got before the war.
And things were different.
Because there was a shortage of labor.
There was a big demand for, for everything.
So people got what they wanted, the demands.
The work conditions improved.
The hours were shorter.
You started, say, 8 o'clock to 6 o'clock and finished.
Whereas before the war, you'd finish-- you'd work, say, from 8 o'clock to 8 o'clock at night.
And Saturday as well, half day.
Well, it was similar to before.
You couldn't get, like, new radios.
Because everything went to war [INAUDIBLE].
And television was just beginning to come out.
The black and white television was just beginning.
Of course, there was television before the war, on a small scale.
But when war broke out, it was stopped.
Because they reckoned the Germans could pick up the positions on the wavelengths.
So transmissions stopped.
Well, you used to go dancing, that's all.
Or there was like social clubs, where you'd go and play draughts or table tennis, or something like that.
Not like today, the cafes, yeah, like the wine bars, they call them?
No, you didn't have none of that.
It influenced a lot, a lot of people.
Because they said, if things like that happen, a lot of us say, where was God in the camps that he allowed those things like that to happen?
It's a difficult question to answer.
Some people believe in it.
Other people don't believe in anything.
A person that will say, if they say they don't believe in anything, you can't blame them.
When you've got a wife and children, or all your family wiped out, they all say, where, where was your God?
You see, it says.
It's written somewhere, that God instructed Abraham to kill his son.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And just as he's about to kill him, he told him to stop.
So it says, from that day, they work out that it was God's command he should kill his son.
And he was quite willing to do it.
And this was God's command that this should happen.
But it was Reverend [INAUDIBLE]-- I think it was Reverend [INAUDIBLE] who said it.
He  said, whether it's God's command or not, we're not going to let these people as they are.
We're going to do everything we can to save them.
As I said, I had a cousin in Paris.
And about a year after we come back, we went to see him.
And she had come from Poland through Germany into France.
She had an aunt and uncle.
She found an aunt that got married.
She also lost a, a husband and children.
And he lost all his family.
So they got married in Germany.
And they were living in Paris.
So we want to see them.
And that's how we met.
So I said-- well, there's not much-- of course, they were in Paris.
And the working conditions were difficult.
They couldn't speak the language.
They'd gone there with nothing.
So I said, well, if you come over to  London, so at least you've got a base or something.
All right, let's hold it there.
